summary:
The development of a practical incandescent electric light bulb by Thomas Edison in 1879 — and its rapid commercialization in the early 1880s — was a pivotal moment in technological history that transformed the relationship between human activity and time, enabling productive work, social life, and entertainment to extend reliably into the night for the first time on a mass scale.

Edison was not the only inventor working on incandescent lighting — Joseph Swan in Britain developed a similar device contemporaneously and the two eventually merged their British interests — but Edison's achievement went significantly beyond inventing a bulb. He conceptualized and built an integrated electrical system: a central generating station (the Pearl Street Station in lower Manhattan, opened in 1882), distribution wiring, metering, and a variety of electrical appliances all designed to work together. This systems-engineering approach was Edison's distinctive contribution and set the template for electrical utilities.

The commercial rollout of electric lighting transformed urban life. Gas lighting, which had illuminated city streets and wealthy homes since the early nineteenth century, was gradually displaced. Factories could operate safely after dark. Department stores and entertainment venues extended their hours. The social and economic consequences were enormous: nighttime became a productive, commercial, and social space in a way it had never been before.

Edison's direct-current (DC) electrical system was soon challenged by the alternating-current (AC) system promoted by George Westinghouse and developed by Nikola Tesla, which proved more efficient for long-distance power transmission. The 'War of Currents' between the two systems in the late 1880s and early 1890s was won by AC for power distribution, though DC continued to be used in various applications. The resulting electrical grid that spread across the industrialized world in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries was the infrastructure of the [[event:2583|Second Industrial Revolution]].
